---
title: "How many people are we talking about when we evaluate the impact of conservation on local populations in low income countries? Estimations from globally gridded population datasets"
author: "Florent Bédécarrats"
abstract: ""
format: 
  html: 
    toc: true
    embed-resources: true
  docx: 
    reference-doc: "data/Word format template.docx"
  typst:
    citeproc: true
editor_options: 
  chunk_output_type: console
execute: 
  warning: false
  error: false
bibliography: references.bib
bibliographystyle: chicago-author-date
bibliographytitle: none
section-numbering: 1.1.a
crossref:
  tbl-title: ""
---

# Introduction

Protected areas (PAs) are a primary tool for biodiversity conservation, established primarily to achieve ecological purposes [@maxwell2020]. However, the impacts of conservation on local well-being is debated [@adams2004]. The amount of population whose living contitions is potentially affected by PAs is a critical issue for conservation planning, determining the distribution of benefits and burdens across social groups, shaping both local reception and long-term feasibility. This is particularly relevant in low- and lower-middle-income countries, where rural households represent the majority of the population (64% in 2022) and account for a large share of global poverty [@castañeda2018]. Recent reductions in poverty rates in developing countries have largely resulted from declines in rural poverty [@worldde], underscoring the importance of understanding how conservation policies intersect with rural livelihoods. 

A large body of the literature emphasizes that the social effects of protected areas are spatially concentrated among populations living inside protected areas or in their immediate surroundings. In their global assessment of social and conservation outcomes, Oldekop et al. [@oldekop2016] restrict their review to studies examining populations living within protected areas or within 10 km of protected area boundaries, drawing on earlier work suggesting that social impacts of protected areas attenuate rapidly with distance [@west2006]. This framing establishes spatial proximity as a central organizing principle for defining “local populations,” but it does not attempt to quantify the population sizes implied by these definitions.

Empirical impact evaluations operationalize locality in heterogeneous ways. As documented by Kandel et al. [@kandel2022], among the 30 counterfactual based studies they synthesize, 10 define exposure through in–out comparisons between populations living inside protected areas and those living outside. Eleven studies rely on administrative units that overlap with or contain protected areas. Nine studies define exposure using explicit distance based criteria. Within this latter group, distance thresholds vary across studies and are sometimes used to define treatment groups, sometimes to exclude nearby populations from control groups. Across all designs, however, the demographic scale of the populations defined as exposed is rarely reported.

A small number of recent studies extend the analysis to global scale in developing countries by explicitly defining local populations through spatial proximity. Naidoo et al. [@naidoo2019] assess well being outcomes across 34 low and middle income countries using Demographic and Health Surveys, classifying populations according to their distance to protected area boundaries. They assume that people living within 10 km of a protected area are close enough to be affected by its presence, an assumption they justify on empirical and theoretical grounds and assess through sensitivity analyses. Similarly, Fisher et al. [@fisher_2024] use Afrobarometer data to evaluate social well being across Africa, again defining exposure based on distance to protected area boundaries. Although these studies differ in data sources and analytical approaches, they share a common reliance on spatial proximity to delineate populations living outside but near protected areas.

Across this literature, two exposure logics therefore coexist. Some studies focus on populations living inside protected areas, while others define local populations as those living outside but within a given distance of protected area boundaries. In both cases, the exposure definitions are explicit, but their demographic implications remain largely implicit. Neither global studies nor most local impact evaluations report how many people live inside protected areas or within commonly used distance based exposure zones, nor how these numbers vary across countries, protected area categories, or over time.

This paper addresses that gap by shifting attention from impacts to magnitudes. Rather than estimating welfare effects, it quantifies how many people live inside protected areas and how many live in their immediate surroundings under commonly used spatial definitions in developing countries, and how these population magnitudes have evolved since 2000. By making the demographic scale of “local populations” explicit, the paper provides a descriptive baseline that complements existing impact evaluations and helps situate their findings in demographic terms.
